attack_chain.description: The attacker obtained a compromised GitHub access token belonging to a Kiln infrastructure engineer, granting them initial access to Kiln's GitHub environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the entry point of the attack was the compromise of a GitHub access token belonging to a Kiln infrastructure engineer

attack_chain.description: Using the stolen GitHub token, the attacker created and immediately deleted branches in the IaC repository to trigger GitHub Actions CI/CD workflows, with branch changes spanning a large number of files to evade detection.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The malicious actor created and immediately deleted branches to trigger CI/CD (Continuous Integration/Deployment) workflows in our IaC (infrastructure as code) repository. The branches changed a very large number of files to remain hidden.

attack_chain.description: From the triggered CI workflows, the attacker harvested stored secrets and cloud credentials (AWS and GCP service account credentials) embedded in the workflow environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: From the triggered CI workflows, they harvested stored secrets and cloud credentials from these workflows.

attack_chain.technique_name: Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token
attack_chain.description: The harvested credentials were used to authenticate to cloud service accounts and gain access to production systems across AWS, GCP, and bare-metal infrastructure.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: These credentials granted access to cloud service accounts and production systems across Amazon Web Services (AWS), Google Cloud Platform (GCP), and our bare-metal infrastructure.

attack_chain.description: The attacker injected a malicious payload directly into a running Kubernetes pod hosting the Kiln Connect API, modifying the API endpoint logic to return a malicious transaction in addition to the expected 'deactivate stake' transaction — without writing any persistent files or modifying container images.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The threat actor injected a malicious payload into a running Kubernetes pod hosting the Kiln Connect API, modifying the logic of a Kiln API endpoint to return a malicious transaction, in addition to the expected 'deactivate stake' transaction.

attack_chain.description: Throughout the attack, the threat actor operated stealthily by never writing persistent files, never modifying code repositories or container images, and routing commands through thousands of different IP addresses to avoid detection.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The threat actor avoided leaving traces by never writing persistent files, never modifying code in repositories, and never modifying container images or databases. Instead, they relied on stealthy commands run directly inside short-lived workloads in the cloud. To further cover their tracks, these commands were launched from thousands of different IP addresses.

attack_chain.description: The malicious transaction returned by the compromised API changed the withdrawal authority of a Solana stake account (only triggered when the stake balance exceeded 150k SOL). A Kiln enterprise customer signed the transaction via their custody solution, resulting in loss of funds.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The malicious transaction changed the withdrawal authority of the Solana stakes, only if the existing withdrawal authority of the stake account provided in the POST call held stake balances above 150k SOL... it was approved by a quorum of signatories in the customer's custody instance... causing a loss of funds.

initial_access_vector: Compromised GitHub personal access token belonging to a Kiln infrastructure engineer, obtained through unknown means
persistence_mechanism: No traditional persistence; attacker operated transiently via short-lived CI/CD workflow executions and in-memory runtime injection into a Kubernetes pod, deliberately avoiding any persistent artifacts
privilege_escalation: Harvested stored cloud credentials (AWS and GCP service account keys) from GitHub Actions CI workflow secrets, then used those credentials to access cloud service accounts and Kubernetes production workloads
